title: Understanding Distributed Systems
subtitle: What every developer should know about large distributed applications
author: Roberto Vitillo
description:
An approachable, well-illustrated guide to the concepts every
software engineer needs to work with large distributed applications.
Roberto Vitillo, a principal engineer at Microsoft, distills decades of
practitioner experience into five clear pillars — communication,
coordination, scalability, resiliency, and observability — using the
same plain prose and hand-drawn diagrams that made his long-running
blog series, "What every developer should know...", a touchstone for
engineers entering the field.
keyIdeas: Communication — request/reply (HTTP, gRPC) and message-passing (queues, pub/sub)
Discovery, leader election, and consensus are coordination primitives
Distributed locks require fencing tokens, not just mutual exclusion
Sharding and replication trade consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
Load balancers and CDNs move work closer to users and smooth traffic
Caching is the most leveraged scalability tool — and the most dangerous
Retries without jitter cause thundering herds; idempotency makes them safe
Circuit breakers, bulkheads, and rate limiters contain failure blast radius
Deadlines and timeouts must be propagated, not invented at each hop
Metrics, logs, and traces answer different questions about the same system
Process supervision, back-pressure, and graceful degradation beat heroic oncall
Distributed systems are a problem of language, vocabulary, and shared mental models
keyTakeaways: Treat every remote call as a failure waiting to happen: timeouts, retries, and idempotency are the baseline, not an optimization.
Choose between consistency, availability, and partition tolerance consciously — and tell your team which one you chose.
Push idempotency into the receiving system. Caller-side retry without server-side idempotency guarantees duplicate work and corrupt state.
Always use jittered exponential backoff for retries. Synchronized retries across N clients turn a blip into an outage.
A cache is not a database. It is a database that lies to you sometimes. Decide in advance what your system does on cache miss, stale read, and eviction.
Sharding keys are forever. A bad shard key causes hot spots that no amount of hardware fixes without a painful re-shard.
Leader election is for coordination, not for performance. A leader is a single point of failure until it steps down cleanly.
Distributed locks without fencing tokens are unsafe — the holder may be paused, network-partitioned, or replaced.
Observability is a product feature. Logs, metrics, and traces must be designed in from day one, not bolted on when production breaks.
Resiliency is the discipline of choosing which requests to refuse when the system is under stress. The default must be fail-safe, not fail-open.
whoShouldRead: Backend and platform engineers designing or operating distributed services
Senior software engineers preparing for system-design interviews
Tech leads and architects making build-vs-buy and consistency trade-offs
Cloud and SRE practitioners who need a shared vocabulary for failure modes
Senior CS students transitioning from single-process coursework to production systems
Engineering managers who want a current, opinionated map of the distributed-systems landscape
